x-enumeration-literals.name: Mark-to-Market Method
x-enumeration-literals.description:
Values the position based on current market prices. Reflects the real-time economic value of assets or liabilities.

Comment:
Often used for Stocks, bonds, derivatives traded in liquid markets.

Example:
A portfolio holds 200 shares of a company.
Current market price = $50 per share
Position value = 200 × 50 = $10,000

x-enumeration-literals.name: Mark-to-Model Method
x-enumeration-literals.description:
Values positions using financial models when market prices are unavailable or illiquid.

Comment:
Often used for Illiquid derivatives, structured notes, exotic options.

Example:
A derivative contract has no active market.
A pricing model estimates its value as $15,000.
Position value = $15,000

x-enumeration-literals.name: Amortized Cost Method
x-enumeration-literals.description:
Values positions based on initial cost adjusted over time using amortization or interest accrual.

Comment:
Often used for Corporate bonds, loans.

Example:
A bond purchased for $10,000 with 5% annual interest, held for 1 year.
Position value = $10,000 + interest accrued = $10,500

x-enumeration-literals.name: Fair Value Method
x-enumeration-literals.description:
Represents the price at which an orderly transaction would occur between knowledgeable market participants.

Comment:
Often used for Securities and derivatives in regulated financial reporting.

Example:
A bond is valued based on current bid-ask prices in a regulated exchange.
Fair value = $10,200

x-enumeration-literals.name: Historical Cost Method
x-enumeration-literals.description:
Uses the original transaction cost of the position, ignoring current market fluctuations.

Comment:
Often used for accounting or internal reporting for stable, long-term assets.

Example:
Stock purchased at $8,000, market price now $9,000.
Historical cost valuation = $8,000

x-enumeration-literals.name: Net Asset Value Method
x-enumeration-literals.description:
Values positions in a fund or pooled investment based on the total assets minus liabilities, divided by shares outstanding

Comment:
Often used for Mutual funds, ETFs, investment funds.

Example:
Fund total assets = $1,000,000, liabilities = $100,000, outstanding shares = 90,000
NAV per share = ($1,000,000 − $100,000) / 90,000 = $10
